market_context: Markets face volatility from earnings season start, geopolitical tensions impacting energy prices, and recent weak economic data including a Q1 S&P 500 decline of 4.63%, February job losses of 92,000, and a widening trade deficit to $57.3B. Fed rates steady at 3.5-3.75% with next meeting April 28-29; personal income dipped 0.1% in February. Energy sector led Q1 gains at +37.91% amid broader equity pressures.

predictions.reasoning: Q1 S&P 500 fell 4.63% amid AI disruption fears and geopolitics, with technology sector down 7.57%.[1] Today's personal income drop of 0.1% and widening trade deficit signal weakening economy.[3] Earnings season and volatility likely extend recent downward pressure.

Nasdaq Composite declined 7.11% in Q1, with tech under steady pressure at -7.57% due to AI concerns.[1] Geopolitical tensions and rising yields add to equity volatility as noted in April commentary.[2] Pre-earnings caution likely weighs on Nasdaq-heavy ETF.

Energy sector surged +37.91% in Q1, standout amid broad declines.[1] Renewed geopolitical tensions visibly influencing markets through energy prices.[2] Continued strength expected despite overall market choppiness.

Dow lost 3.58% in Q1, less severe than S&P/Nasdaq, with industrials up 4.55%.[1] Mixed economic signals like steady CPI and job losses balanced by wage growth.[1] Volatility from earnings and Fed watch may keep Dow range-bound.
